title: Linked lists - each piece points to the next
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: A linked list is a chain of nodes, each holding a value and a link to the next node. There's no index - you follow the links.
explanation: Where an array is one solid block, a linked list is scattered: each node knows only its own value and where the next one lives. That makes adding to the front cheap - just point a new node at the old head - but it means there's no jumping. To reach the 100th item you walk the chain, link by link, from the start. Different trade than an array: easy to grow, slow to reach into.
code_example:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.next = None

head = Node(1)
head.next = Node(2)
print(head.next.val)

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: head holds 1, and head.next is the second node.

exercises.question: To reach the 100th item in a linked list, you must...
options: jump straight to it by index
follow the links one by one from the start
read it instantly like an array
hints: There's no index here - no slot numbers.
Each node only knows the next one.
You walk the chain, link by link.

exercises.question:
Each node holds a value and a link to the ___ node.

_
hints: It's the field in the Node class.
The one it points forward to.
